shots.speech_text: Imagine Silicon burning in massive stars (>8 M☉) occurs at T ~3×10⁹ . That single number is the key to understanding Stellar nucleosynthesis: silicon and oxygen in sand-forming minerals — one of the most extraordinary phenomena in the known universe.

shots.speech_text: The key measurement: Silicon burning in massive stars (>8 M☉) occurs at T ~3×10⁹ K (~0.26 MeV) over ~1 day; Si-28 and O-16 solar abundances 7.10×10⁵ and 1.41×10⁷ relative to H=10¹² (Lodders, 2003, ApJ 591). Results like this, produced by California Institute of Technology (Caltech) — W.K. Kellogg Radiation Laboratory, transformed our understanding of Stellar nucleosynthesis: silicon and oxygen in sand-forming minerals.

shots.speech_text: We are only beginning to understand Stellar nucleosynthesis: silicon and oxygen in sand-forming minerals. And perhaps that's exactly the point — the universe keeps its most intricate secrets just within reach, if we keep looking.

guardrails: HELIX is voiceover only — no on-screen host, no synthetic presenter, no avatar.
All quantitative claims must include units and citation.
Uncertainty must be stated where present (e.g. 'approximately', 'within a margin of').
Do not present theoretical models as confirmed fact without qualification.
Do not fabricate measurements, paper titles, DOIs, or institutional affiliations.
Maintain wonder without hyperbole — the phenomena are extraordinary enough.
No speculation presented as established science.
HELIX does not cover history, biography, or language — those are DAVID's domain.
